questions.stem: You are pairing on JHotDraw and spot a method 'EllipseFigure.draw()' whose body is 40 lines of tangled drawing logic. Your teammate wants the smallest correct first move. What do you apply?

options.text: Extract Method to break the body into named sub-steps
Move Method to relocate it onto DrawingView
Inline Method to fold it into its caller
Extract Superclass to share it with other figures

questions.explanation: A 40-line body is a Long Method; Extract Method packages groups of statements into named units and is the canonical first move. Move/Inline/Extract Superclass address different smells. (Refactoring1 p.11, 127)

questions.stem: A 'RectangleTool' method seems to read and write the fields of 'Rectangle' far more than its own. As the maintainer, which refactoring removes this Feature Envy?

options.text: Introduce Parameter Object on its arguments
Move Method, relocating it to Rectangle

questions.explanation: A method more interested in another class than its own is Feature Envy; Move Method puts it where the data lives. The other moves target Long Parameter List, Switch Statements, and field exposure respectively. (Refactoring1 p.15, 142)

questions.stem: During a code review you find a class 'DrawingAttributes' that holds rendering state plus persistence plus undo bookkeeping. Which refactoring best starts splitting these responsibilities?

options.text: Inline Class into its primary client
Extract Class, moving one responsibility's fields/methods out

questions.explanation: One class doing the work of two is Large Class; Extract Class peels a coherent responsibility into a new class. Inline/Collapse merge classes (wrong direction); Parameterize Method targets duplication. (Refactoring1 p.16, 149)

questions.stem: You inherit a 'Toolbar' method that builds a string by appending in a loop, but it relies on five interlocking local variables so Extract Method keeps failing. What do you do?

options.text: Force Extract Method and pass all five as parameters
Inline the method and abandon decomposition
Apply Hide Delegate to the loop body
Replace Method with Method Object so locals become fields

questions.explanation: When tangled locals block Extract Method, Replace Method with Method Object turns the method into an object whose fields are the former locals, enabling decomposition. The others don't resolve the local-variable obstacle. (Refactoring1 p.13, 135)

questions.stem: A change request asks you to add a new figure type to JHotDraw. The target Figure hierarchy is tangled. Before writing any new code you clean it so the addition slots in easily. Which phase are you in?
questions.explanation: Cleaning the change region BEFORE injecting new behaviour, adding no functionality, is Prefactoring (Fowler's 'refactor when you add function'). Postfactoring is afterward; Actualization adds the behaviour. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: You finished adding a new tool to JHotDraw; it works, but it left a 25-line method and a duplicated block. Before concluding the task you remove both. Which phase is this?
questions.explanation: Removing smells the change introduced, after the feature works, is Postfactoring (the Boy-Scout rule; BetterCode G10). Prefactoring happens before; Actualization is the functional change itself. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: A client class navigates 'order.getCustomer().getAddress().getCity()'. As maintainer you want to shield the client from the structure. Which refactoring fits?

options.text: Remove Middle Man so the client calls each object directly
Hide Delegate, adding a method on order that returns the city

questions.explanation: The Message Chain is broken by Hide Delegate: add a server method so the client stops walking the chain. Remove Middle Man does the opposite; the other two address unmodifiable libraries and arrays. (Refactoring1 p.18, 157)

questions.stem: Reviewing a payment module you find 'switch(type)' branches repeated in three methods, each choosing behaviour by an account-type code. Which single Fowler refactoring addresses the root smell?

options.text: Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses

questions.explanation: Type-switching logic is the Switch Statements smell; Replace Conditional with Polymorphism moves each leg into a subclass override. Consolidate/Decompose/Guard Clauses tidy conditionals but keep the type switch. (Refactoring1 p.34, 255)

questions.stem: You find a method signature 'render(x, y, width, height, color, opacity, rotation)'. The maintainer asks for the least-churn fix to the Long Parameter List where several args group naturally. Which?

options.text: Introduce Parameter Object grouping the geometry/style
Replace Parameter with Method for all seven
Add a builder for the whole class

questions.explanation: Parameters that naturally travel together (a Data Clump) are bundled by Introduce Parameter Object, which also satisfies BetterCode G4. Replace Parameter with Method only helps args the receiver can recompute. (Refactoring1 p.41, 295)

questions.stem: In the lab you open JHotDraw, run SonarLint, and it flags a 60-line method. Following the prescribed workflow, what is your very next deliverable step?

options.text: Commit the fix directly to main
Create a feature branch, then apply suitable Kerievsky refactorings

questions.explanation: The lab workflow is: feature branch -> SonarLint to find smells -> apply Kerievsky refactorings -> document the strategy. Committing to main, rewriting, or muting the rule all violate the workflow. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: A junior says 'I'll just rewrite this 200-line God class from scratch over the weekend.' Per the lecture's craft discipline, what should you advise instead?

options.text: Rewrite is always preferable for large classes
Add comments to explain the class
Apply a sequence of small behaviour-preserving refactorings

questions.explanation: Refactoring proceeds as small behaviour-preserving steps, keeping the system working after each, which a from-scratch rewrite abandons. Comments and deleting tests address neither structure nor safety. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: A 'ConnectionHandle' subclass overrides nearly every inherited method and ignores the parent's data. As maintainer you decide inheritance was the wrong tool. Which refactoring do you apply?
questions.explanation: A subclass that rejects most of its bequest (Refused Bequest) is fixed by Replace Inheritance with Delegation: hold the former superclass in a field. The inverse and the other moves don't sever the bad inheritance. (Refactoring1 p.49, 352)

questions.stem: You and a teammate disagree: a method both saves a record AND returns its new id. They want one method; you cite Command-Query Separation. Which refactoring resolves it your way?

options.text: Separate Query from Modifier, splitting into two methods

questions.explanation: A method that returns a value and changes state violates Command-Query Separation; Separate Query from Modifier splits it into a query and a modifier. The others target parameters and field access. (Refactoring1 p.39, 279)

questions.stem: While finishing a task you notice a method littered with comments that merely re-explain confusing code. Applying the Comments-smell guidance, what is the correct fix?

options.text: Delete every comment in the file
Extract Method and give it a self-documenting name
Add more comments until it is clear
Leave it; comments are always good

questions.explanation: The Comments smell means comments compensate for unclear code; the fix is Extract Method plus a good name so the comment becomes unnecessary, not blanket deletion. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Refactoring a JHotDraw 'AbstractTool', you find two subclass methods perform the same steps in the same order but with different details. You want to pull the skeleton up. Which refactoring?
questions.explanation: Identical step-ordering with varying details across subclasses is fixed by Form Template Method: align signatures so the skeleton can be pulled up (the Template Method pattern). The others don't unify the algorithm. (Refactoring1 p.47, 345)

questions.stem: Your team keeps copy-pasting a 12-line validation block. You decide to act on the Rule of Three. What does that rule tell you to do here?

options.text: Wait until a fourth copy appears
Copy it once more, then stop
Refactor now, since this is the third similar occurrence

questions.explanation: The Rule of Three says refactor the third time you do something similar (first do it, second wince, third refactor). Waiting for a fourth or copying again ignores the rule. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT behaviour-preserving and therefore NOT a refactoring?

options.text: Adding a new validation rule that rejects previously-accepted input
Renaming a private method and its callers
Extracting a code fragment into a well-named helper
Moving a field to the class that uses it most

questions.explanation: Rejecting previously-valid input changes observable behaviour, so it is a modification, not a refactoring. Renaming, extracting, and moving are all behaviour-preserving. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: Which item is NOT one of Fowler's 22 code smells?
questions.explanation: Cyclomatic complexity is a BetterCode/SIG metric (G2), not a named Fowler smell. Feature Envy, Data Clumps, and Refused Bequest are in Fowler's list of 22. (Refactoring1 p.6-8)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of Fowler's seven refactoring categories?
questions.explanation: Measuring metrics is BetterCode/SIG territory, not a Fowler category. The seven are Composing Methods, Moving Features, Organizing Data, Simplifying Conditionals, Making Method Calls Simpler, Dealing with Generalization, Big Refactorings. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of BetterCode Guideline 10's seven 'How To' clean-code rules?

options.text: Keep cyclomatic complexity at or below 5

questions.explanation: The CC ceiling belongs to G2, not the G10 'How To' list. G10's rules cover unit smells, bad comments, code in comments, dead code, long identifiers, magic constants, and badly handled exceptions. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a benefit Kerievsky attributes to composite (high-level) refactorings?

options.text: They provide an overall plan for a refactoring sequence
They give insight into implementing patterns

questions.explanation: Composite refactorings improve structure, not speed; Kerievsky's three benefits are an overall plan, non-obvious directions, and pattern-implementation insight. (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: Which statement about code smells is FALSE?

options.text: A smell is a surface indication of a possible deeper problem
A code smell is by definition a bug that must be fixed immediately
A smell suggests a candidate refactoring

questions.explanation: A smell is a symptom, not a defect, and does not always require action; calling it 'by definition a bug' is the classic trap. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which statement about Prefactoring is FALSE?

options.text: It adds the new functionality required by the change
It aims to turn a hard change into an easy one

questions.explanation: Prefactoring adds no functionality; it is pure behaviour-preserving restructuring done before Actualization to ease the upcoming change. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the four SIG levels at which the guidelines operate?
questions.explanation: 'Package-private' is a Java access modifier, not a SIG level. The four levels are Unit, Module, Component, System. (BetterCode p.9)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a valid inverse pair of refactorings?

options.text: Replace Inheritance with Delegation <-> Replace Delegation with Inheritance

questions.explanation: Extract Method's inverse is Inline Method, not Move Method. The other three are genuine inverse pairs. (Refactoring1 p.12)

questions.stem: Which claim about tests and refactoring is FALSE?

options.text: Tests act as a safety net that verifies behaviour preservation
Because refactoring is behaviour-preserving by definition, running tests is unnecessary
Lack of tests makes refactoring unsafe
BetterCode G9 'Automated Tests' supports the refactoring discipline

questions.explanation: Behaviour preservation must be verified, not assumed; tests are the safety net, which is why G9 exists. Claiming tests are unnecessary is wrong practice. (BetterCode p.53)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a smell that appears only in Kerievsky's catalog (i.e. it is also a Fowler smell)?
questions.explanation: Primitive Obsession is in Fowler's 22 (and Kerievsky cross-lists it). Combinatorial Explosion, Solution Sprawl, and Oddball Solution are Kerievsky-only. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12-13)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a motivation BetterCode gives for short units (G1)?

options.text: They run faster at execution time

questions.explanation: G1's motivations are testability, analyzability, and reusability, not runtime speed; maintainability guidelines target human cost, not performance. (BetterCode p.7)

questions.stem: How do Divergent Change and Shotgun Surgery differ?

options.text: Divergent Change = one class changed for many reasons; Shotgun Surgery = one change touches many classes
Divergent Change = one change touches many classes; Shotgun Surgery = one class changed for many reasons
Both mean the same thing under different names
Both describe excessive delegation between classes

questions.explanation: They are mirror images: Divergent Change is one class with many unrelated reasons to change; Shotgun Surgery is one conceptual change scattered across many classes. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Middle Man and Message Chains are said to pull in opposite directions. Which contrast is correct?

options.text: Both describe a class that forwards every call
Middle Man = too much delegation; Message Chains = too much exposed navigation through objects
Middle Man = long navigation chains; Message Chains = excessive delegation
Both are fixed by the same single refactoring

questions.explanation: Middle Man (excessive delegation) and Message Chains (long a.getB().getC() navigation) are opposite imbalances, so Remove Middle Man and Hide Delegate pull in opposite directions. (Refactoring1 p.8, 318)

questions.stem: What is the key difference between high-level (Kerievsky) and low-level (Fowler) refactorings?

options.text: Low-level refactorings introduce patterns; high-level ones are single mechanical edits
High-level refactorings change behaviour while low-level ones do not
High-level refactorings are composite sequences that introduce a design pattern; low-level ones are atomic, often tool-automatable moves
There is no difference; the terms are synonyms

questions.explanation: Fowler's are atomic, mechanical, often automatable; Kerievsky's are composite sequences of those moves that introduce a GoF pattern. Both are behaviour-preserving. (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: Given that both are behaviour-preserving and use identical catalogs/metrics, what is the ONE axis on which Prefactoring and Postfactoring differ?

options.text: Whether they use the Fowler catalog
Their position relative to Actualization (before vs after the functional change)

questions.explanation: Both are behaviour-preserving and use the same catalogs/metrics; the only difference is position around Actualization. (Refactoring1 p.5; BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: You suspect either Hide Delegate or Remove Middle Man is needed. Which symptom should drive that decision?

options.text: By which smell dominates: too little hiding (Message Chains) favours Hide Delegate; too much delegation (Middle Man) favours Remove Middle Man
Pick whichever produces fewer lines of code

questions.explanation: They tug opposite ways, so the dominant smell decides: Message Chains/Inappropriate Intimacy -> Hide Delegate; Middle Man -> Remove Middle Man. You can't keep both fully applied. (Refactoring1 p.18, 160)

questions.stem: Extract Class and Inline Class are inverses. When does each apply?

options.text: Extract Class for a Lazy Class; Inline Class for a Large Class
Extract Class for a Large Class doing too much; Inline Class for a Lazy Class doing too little
Both apply to Long Parameter List

questions.explanation: Extract Class splits an over-stuffed class (Large Class); Inline Class folds an under-used one (Lazy Class) into another. They are direct opposites. (Refactoring1 p.16, 149-154)

questions.stem: Guideline 5 (module coupling) and Guideline 6 (component coupling) both target loose coupling. How do they differ in level?

options.text: G5 is at System level; G6 is at Unit level
Both operate at the Unit level
G5 operates at the Module level (fan-in to a file/class); G6 operates at the Component level (hidden-code % across top-level components)
G5 measures parameters; G6 measures lines of code

questions.explanation: G5 is Module-level coupling measured by fan-in; G6 is Component-level coupling measured by the weighted percentage of hidden code. (BetterCode p.5, 34, 39)

questions.stem: Introduce Parameter Object versus Replace Parameter with Method: which fits which situation?

options.text: Parameter Object when an arg is recomputable; Replace Parameter with Method when args group
Both only apply to switch statements
Both only apply to null checks
Parameter Object when several args naturally group; Replace Parameter with Method when the receiver can compute an arg itself

questions.explanation: Group naturally-clustered args with Introduce Parameter Object; drop an arg the receiver can recompute with Replace Parameter with Method. Both shrink Long Parameter Lists differently. (Refactoring1 p.41, 292-295)

questions.stem: For the Switch Statements smell, how do Fowler's and Kerievsky's prescribed fixes compare?

options.text: Fowler: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism; Kerievsky: Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command or Move Accumulation to Visitor
Fowler prescribes Command; Kerievsky prescribes plain polymorphism

questions.explanation: Fowler's low-level fix is Replace Conditional with Polymorphism; Kerievsky's pattern-directed fixes are Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command and Move Accumulation to Visitor. (Refactoring1 p.34; HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: Compare Extract Method and Replace Method with Method Object: when is the latter needed?

options.text: When the method is already short and clear
When entangled local variables make plain Extract Method impossible
When the method has no local variables
When you want to inline the method

questions.explanation: Replace Method with Method Object is the escalation used when interlocking locals block Extract Method; it turns those locals into fields so decomposition becomes possible. (Refactoring1 p.13, 135)

questions.stem: Introduce Foreign Method versus Introduce Local Extension both target Incomplete Library Class. What decides which to use?

options.text: Whether you can edit the library (both require editing it)
The number of missing methods: one -> Foreign Method; several -> Local Extension
The cyclomatic complexity of the library

questions.explanation: Both handle an unmodifiable library class: Introduce Foreign Method adds a single helper in the client; Introduce Local Extension (subclass/wrapper) is for several needed methods. (Refactoring1 p.20, 162-164)

questions.stem: Compare the Data Class smell with the Lazy Class smell. Which contrast holds?

options.text: Both mean a class doing too much
Data Class = excessive delegation; Lazy Class = long parameter lists
Data Class = fields plus only getters/setters (anaemic); Lazy Class = a class not doing enough to justify its existence

questions.explanation: A Data Class is an anaemic field-bag with only accessors; a Lazy Class earns too little to justify maintenance (fix: Inline Class / Collapse Hierarchy). (Refactoring1 p.7-8)

questions.stem: Symptom: every time you add a subclass to one hierarchy you must add a matching subclass to a second hierarchy. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: Mandatory paired subclassing across two hierarchies is Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies (fixed by Tease Apart Inheritance). (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Symptom: an instance variable is set and meaningful only during one particular operation and is null the rest of the time. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: A field set only in certain circumstances is a Temporary Field (often fixed with Extract Class or Extract Subclass). (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Symptom: a class exists whose every public method simply forwards to a held object, adding nothing of its own. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: Excessive delegation with no added value is Middle Man, fixed by Remove Middle Man. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Symptom: classes and configuration hooks were added 'in case we need them later,' but nothing uses them. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: Features added for a need that may arise someday (a YAGNI violation) are Speculative Generality. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Symptom: two unrelated classes reach deep into each other's internals and share private knowledge. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: Excessive mutual coupling and shared internals is Inappropriate Intimacy. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Before: a method has five parameters {street, city, zip, country, region}. After: it takes one 'Address' object. Name the refactoring applied.
questions.explanation: Folding a clump of related parameters into one object is Introduce Parameter Object (also the G4 fix). (Refactoring1 p.41, 295)

questions.stem: Before: 'class Engineer extends Employee' but Engineer overrides 90% of Employee. After: Engineer holds an Employee field and delegates. Name the refactoring.
questions.explanation: Swapping a misused superclass for a held field plus delegation is Replace Inheritance with Delegation (fixes Refused Bequest). (Refactoring1 p.49, 352)

questions.stem: Before: 'if (shape.type == CIRCLE) {...} else if (...SQUARE...)'. After: each shape subclass overrides 'area()'. Name the refactoring.
questions.explanation: Moving each branch of a type switch into a subclass override is Replace Conditional with Polymorphism. (Refactoring1 p.34, 255)

questions.stem: Before: client calls 'department.getManager().getName()'. After: client calls 'department.getManagerName()'. Name the refactoring.
questions.explanation: Adding a server method so the client stops walking the chain is Hide Delegate (fixes Message Chains). (Refactoring1 p.18, 157)

questions.stem: Before: a 30-line block computing a tax appears inline. After: it is a call to 'computeTax(...)'. Name the refactoring.
questions.explanation: Packaging a coherent fragment into a named method is Extract Method. (Refactoring1 p.11, 110)

questions.stem: Before: 'int customerType' used as a code with switch logic. After: a small 'CustomerType' object replaces the int. Name the smell that was removed.
questions.explanation: Using a primitive where a small object belongs is Primitive Obsession; Replace Data Value with Object (or Replace Type Code with Class) removes it. (Refactoring1 p.7, 175)

questions.stem: Symptom report from SonarLint: 'this single class is edited in three commits for three completely unrelated reasons each sprint.' Name the smell.
questions.explanation: One class repeatedly changed for unrelated reasons is Divergent Change (single-responsibility violation). (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Symptom: adding one new currency requires edits in eight different classes scattered across the codebase. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: One conceptual change forcing many little edits across many classes is Shotgun Surgery (fixed by Move Method/Field to consolidate). (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Before: two classes 'A' and 'B' each have a 'render()' that begins with the same 8 lines. After: a shared superclass holds those lines. Name the refactoring.
questions.explanation: Hoisting common features from two sibling classes into a new parent is Extract Superclass (fixes Duplicated Code across siblings). (Refactoring1 p.45, 336)

questions.stem: What is the most likely consequence of refactoring a tangled module with no automated tests?

options.text: Cyclomatic complexity is guaranteed to drop
You may silently break observable behaviour with no safety net to catch it
The refactoring becomes behaviour-changing by definition

questions.explanation: Without tests, behaviour preservation is unverified, so regressions can slip through unnoticed; that is why G9 (Automated Tests) underpins safe refactoring. (BetterCode p.53)

questions.stem: A module's fan-in climbs from 8 to 55. Per BetterCode's coupling table, what is the consequence for modifiability/testability?

options.text: Fan-in has no effect on testability
It moves from 'Excellent' into the 'Impossible' band

questions.explanation: Fan-in 1-10 is Excellent; >50 is Impossible. Rising to 55 crosses into the worst band for modifiability/testability. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: What is the consequence of over-applying design patterns (e.g. wrapping everything in a Singleton)?

options.text: It becomes a smell itself, warranting moves AWAY from the pattern like Inline Singleton
It reduces cyclomatic complexity to zero
It is required by the Rule of Three

questions.explanation: Over-applying patterns is itself a smell; Kerievsky's Refactoring Directions include moves away from patterns (e.g. Inline Singleton). The Fowler analog is Speculative Generality. (HighLevelRefactoring p.22)

questions.stem: A unit grows from 14 to 23 lines of code. What is the consequence under BetterCode G1 and the prescribed remedy?

options.text: It still passes G1; no action needed
It violates G1 (15-LOC limit); split it into smaller units via Extract Method
It violates G2 and needs polymorphism
It violates G4 and needs a parameter object

questions.explanation: G1 caps units at 15 LOC; 23 LOC violates it and the remedy is splitting via Extract Method / Replace Method with Method Object. (BetterCode p.7, 11)

questions.stem: A method's branch points rise from 4 to 6. What follows under BetterCode G2?

options.text: It now violates G2 (max 4 branch points; CC = 7), and should be simplified

questions.explanation: G2 caps branch points at 4 (CC <= 5). Six branch points give CC = 7, violating G2; split the unit, e.g. via Replace Conditional with Polymorphism. (BetterCode p.13, 16)

questions.stem: Consequence of skipping Postfactoring after Actualization on every task?

options.text: The new feature stops working immediately
Cyclomatic complexity is forced to zero
Smells accumulate and the codebase steadily degrades, raising future change cost

questions.explanation: Postfactoring removes smells the change introduced; skipping it lets debt accumulate, making later changes harder (the opposite of the Boy-Scout rule). (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: If you compute cyclomatic complexity as just the branch-point count and forget the '+1', what happens?

options.text: You under-report CC by one, possibly passing a unit that actually fails G2
You measure parameters instead of complexity

questions.explanation: CC = branch points + 1; omitting the +1 understates complexity by one and can wrongly clear a borderline unit. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: Consequence per BetterCode of letting a system grow ever larger (violating G8)?

options.text: Higher defect density and greater likelihood the project fails

questions.explanation: BetterCode warns that large systems have higher defect density and that aiming for a large codebase makes failure more likely; hence G8 keeps it small. (BetterCode p.49)

questions.stem: You apply Hide Delegate everywhere to kill message chains. What unintended smell can this cause, and what is the counter-move?

options.text: Long Method; counter with Inline Method
Data Class; counter with Extract Class
Middle Man (over-delegation); counter with Remove Middle Man

questions.explanation: Over-hiding delegates turns the server into a Middle Man; Remove Middle Man swings back. The two refactorings are opposing forces you balance per dominant smell. (Refactoring1 p.18, 160)

questions.stem: Consequence of a method with 7 parameters under BetterCode G4, and the recommended cure?

options.text: It violates G4 (max 4 parameters); extract parameters into an object

questions.explanation: G4 caps parameters at 4; seven violates it and the cure is to extract parameters into objects (Introduce Parameter Object). (BetterCode p.25)

questions.stem: What is the intended consequence of doing Prefactoring before Actualization rather than coding the feature directly into tangled code?

options.text: The hard change becomes an easy one, lowering the risk of the functional edit
The feature ships without any code change

questions.explanation: Prefactoring restructures the change region so the new behaviour slots in easily, turning a hard change into an easy one without adding functionality. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: A method takes a Long Parameter List whose arguments clearly belong together as a concept. What is the BEST refactoring?
questions.explanation: Cohesive parameters (a Data Clump) are best bundled with Introduce Parameter Object. The alternatives target recomputable args, value-only duplication, and conditionals. (Refactoring1 p.41, 295)

questions.stem: Which is the BEST Fowler refactoring to eliminate a type-based switch statement at its root?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism removes the type switch entirely by dispatching through subclasses; the others merely tidy conditional text. (Refactoring1 p.34, 255)

questions.stem: A method has a deeply nested if/else where the normal execution path is hard to see. BEST refactoring?

options.text: Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses

questions.explanation: Guard clauses surface the special cases and clarify the normal path, the prescribed fix for an obscured main flow. (Refactoring1 p.33, 250)

questions.stem: Several conditional tests scattered in a method all lead to the same result. BEST refactoring?
questions.explanation: Combining tests that share one outcome into a single extracted condition is Consolidate Conditional Expression. (Refactoring1 p.33, 240)

questions.stem: Repeated 'if (x == null)' checks litter a class. BEST refactoring?

options.text: Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses

questions.explanation: Introduce Null Object replaces scattered null checks with a polymorphic null object. (Refactoring1 p.36, 260)

questions.stem: A method returns the internal collection directly, letting callers mutate it. BEST refactoring?
questions.explanation: Encapsulate Collection returns a read-only view and exposes add/remove methods, protecting the internal collection. (Refactoring1 p.30, 208)

questions.stem: A complicated if-then-else has hard-to-read condition, then-part, and else-part. BEST refactoring?
questions.explanation: Decompose Conditional extracts methods from each of the condition, then, and else parts to name them. (Refactoring1 p.32, 238)

questions.stem: You need to extend an unmodifiable third-party 'DateUtil' with just one missing helper. BEST refactoring?
questions.explanation: For a single missing method on a class you cannot edit, Introduce Foreign Method adds a client-side helper taking the server instance as first arg. Local Extension is for several methods. (Refactoring1 p.20, 162)

questions.stem: You access a field directly but the coupling has become awkward and you want subclasses to override access. BEST refactoring?
questions.explanation: Self Encapsulate Field creates getters/setters and routes even internal access through them, enabling override. (Refactoring1 p.24, 171)

questions.stem: Among Kerievsky's Long Method remedies, one specifically threads an accumulator through as a Collecting Parameter. Which refactoring is that?
questions.explanation: Move Accumulation to Collecting Parameter is the Kerievsky refactoring that threads a collecting parameter through to gather results. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: Conditional Complexity that selects behaviour at runtime is BEST refactored toward which pattern via 'Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy'?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy moves the branching behaviour into interchangeable Strategy objects. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12-13)

questions.stem: Two subclasses differ only in methods that return constant values. BEST refactoring?
questions.explanation: When subclasses vary only by constant-returning methods, Replace Subclass with Fields turns those into superclass fields and deletes the subclasses. (Refactoring1 p.30, 232)

questions.stem: A class is doing too much largely through many conditional statements selecting special cases. Which BIG refactoring fits best?
questions.explanation: Extract Hierarchy creates a class hierarchy where each subclass represents a special case, the big-refactoring cousin of Replace Conditional with Polymorphism. (Refactoring1 p.57)

questions.stem: A teammate insists 'I refactored the renderer and it now runs 2x faster — speed was the goal.' What is the precise correction?

options.text: Correct: speed is refactoring's defining goal
Refactoring is only about adding features
Refactoring targets understandability/modifiability, not speed; a behaviour-preserving speed change is incidental, not the goal
Refactoring requires the runtime to change

questions.explanation: Fowler's goal is human-facing (easier to understand, cheaper to modify); performance is not the aim of refactoring. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: On the slides you see 'Extract Method (110)' and 'Replace Conditional with Polymorphism (255)'. A student thinks 110 and 255 are slide numbers. What is correct?

options.text: They are page numbers in the source book ([Fowler99]), not slide numbers

questions.explanation: The parenthesised numbers are [Fowler99]/[Ker05] book page numbers printed on the slides, not lecture slide numbers. (Refactoring1 p.11)

questions.stem: Someone argues 'this duplicated block is a smell, so it is a bug we must fix right now.' What's wrong?

options.text: A smell is a symptom, not a bug, and doesn't always demand immediate action
Duplicated Code is not a smell
Smells can never be acted upon

questions.explanation: Smells indicate a possible deeper problem and a candidate refactoring, but they are not defects and not all require action. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: A reviewer says 'the Comments smell means we should strip all comments from the code.' Why is that misconception wrong?

options.text: The Comments smell is about comments compensating for bad code; the fix is Extract Method + a good name, not deleting all comments
The Comments smell means add more comments

questions.explanation: Comments-as-a-smell flags comments masking unclear code; you fix the code (Extract Method + naming) so the comment is unnecessary, not blanket-delete. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: A developer says 'the Rule of Three means I should never duplicate code even once.' Correct them.

options.text: It means duplicate exactly three times always
No: do it the first time, wince and duplicate the second, refactor only the third
It means refactor after the fourth duplication

questions.explanation: The Rule of Three tolerates the first and second occurrence and triggers refactoring on the third similar one. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: A student claims 'Prefactoring and Postfactoring both add a bit of the new feature, just at different times.' What's the misconception?

options.text: Both are purely behaviour-preserving; neither adds functionality — only Actualization does

questions.explanation: Pre- and Postfactoring are both behaviour-preserving restructurings around Actualization, which is the only phase that adds behaviour. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'cyclomatic complexity equals the number of branch points.' What is the precise rule?
questions.explanation: Cyclomatic complexity is branch points plus one; forgetting the +1 understates it. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'more design patterns always means more maintainable.' What does Kerievsky's Refactoring Directions matrix teach instead?

options.text: Patterns can only be added, never removed
You can refactor AWAY from a pattern too; over-application is a smell
Patterns replace the need for tests

questions.explanation: The matrix lists moves both toward and away from each pattern (e.g. Inline Singleton), because over-applying a pattern is itself a smell. (HighLevelRefactoring p.21-22)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'a unit of exactly 15 lines violates G1.' What is correct?

options.text: 15 LOC is the limit, so exactly 15 is compliant; 16+ violates
There is no line limit in G1

questions.explanation: G1 limits units to 15 LOC, so exactly 15 sits at the threshold and passes; only longer units violate. (BetterCode p.7)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'refactoring is safe even without tests because it is behaviour-preserving by definition.' What's the flaw?

options.text: Correct: tests are pointless during refactoring
Refactoring changes behaviour, so tests fail anyway
Behaviour preservation is a goal to be verified, not a guarantee; without tests, mistakes go undetected

questions.explanation: The definition states the intended invariant; humans still err, so tests verify it. That's why G9 pairs with the refactoring discipline. (BetterCode p.53)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Divergent Change and Shotgun Surgery are basically the same smell.' Correct the student.

options.text: They are mirror opposites: one class/many reasons vs one change/many classes
Both are fixed by Introduce Null Object

questions.explanation: Divergent Change = one class altered for many reasons; Shotgun Surgery = one change rippling across many classes. Don't conflate them. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: In Rajlich's eight-phase change process as framed here, which phase comes immediately BEFORE Actualization?
questions.explanation: The spine is ... Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring ...; Prefactoring directly precedes Actualization. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Which phase comes immediately AFTER Actualization in this lecture's framing?
questions.explanation: Actualization is followed by Postfactoring (clean up smells the new code introduced) before Conclusion/Verification. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Lay out this lecture's change-process spine in the order the phases actually execute. Which sequence is right?

options.text: Impact Analysis -> Concept Location -> Postfactoring -> Actualization -> Prefactoring -> Verification
Prefactoring -> Concept Location -> Actualization -> Impact Analysis -> Postfactoring
Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Verification

questions.explanation: The framed spine runs Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Verification. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: A composite refactoring 'Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy' is performed as a sequence of small steps. What is true of that sequence?

options.text: Each step is a small behaviour-preserving move, and the system stays working after each
Only the final step preserves behaviour
The steps may temporarily change observable behaviour
It must be done as one atomic edit

questions.explanation: A composite refactoring is a chain of small behaviour-preserving moves; the system is kept fully working after each step (the algebra of refactoring). (RefactLab p.1; HighLevelRefactoring p.10)

questions.stem: For a JHotDraw 'add a new figure type' request, which Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring flow is correct?

options.text: Add the new Figure -> clean the hierarchy -> never refactor
Clean the Figure/Tool hierarchy -> add the new Figure/Tool -> remove duplication introduced
Remove duplication -> clean the hierarchy -> add the Figure
Add the Figure -> add the Figure again -> verify

questions.explanation: Prefactoring eases the addition, Actualization adds the Figure/Tool, Postfactoring removes any duplication the change introduced. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Order the lab's workflow steps correctly.

options.text: Run SonarLint -> commit to main -> document -> create branch
Apply refactorings -> create branch -> run SonarLint -> identify smells
Create feature branch -> run SonarLint -> identify smells -> apply Kerievsky refactorings -> document strategy
Document strategy -> create branch -> apply refactorings -> run SonarLint

questions.explanation: The lab sequence is: feature branch, SonarLint, find smells in JHotDraw, apply [Ker05] refactorings, document the strategy. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: The disciplined refactoring loop the lecture teaches proceeds in which order?

options.text: Refactor -> measure -> write tests -> identify smell -> stop
Identify smell -> rewrite class -> ship -> measure once
Pick refactoring -> change behaviour -> measure -> commit
Measure -> identify smell -> pick refactoring -> apply in small steps -> re-run tests -> re-measure

questions.explanation: The loop is measure -> identify smell -> pick a catalog refactoring -> apply in small behaviour-preserving steps -> re-run tests -> re-measure, repeated until under threshold. (BetterCode p.10)

questions.stem: Within the change process, which two phases are the 'refactoring bookends' around Actualization?
questions.explanation: Prefactoring (before) and Postfactoring (after) are the two refactoring bookends of Actualization. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Kerievsky says a single composite refactoring provides an 'overall plan.' What does that mean for the ordering of its sub-steps?

options.text: It removes the need for any low-level refactorings
It sequences the small low-level refactorings toward the target pattern in a deliberate order
It lets you skip behaviour preservation

questions.explanation: A composite refactoring lays out an ordered plan of low-level moves that build up to a pattern, which is one of its three benefits. (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: In the lab, what is the role of SonarLint?

options.text: The automated smell detector run in the IDE against JHotDraw

questions.explanation: SonarLint is the lab's IDE smell detector — the automated analog of Fowler's 'Symptoms of Bad Code' and BetterCode's metrics. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: The lab asks you to apply refactorings from which specific catalog to the JHotDraw smells?

options.text: The GoF catalog of patterns only

questions.explanation: The lab explicitly asks you to apply Kerievsky ([Ker05]) refactoring patterns to remove the smells. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Why is Kerievsky's 'refactoring to patterns' especially suited to JHotDraw as a lab target?

options.text: JHotDraw is a GoF-pattern showcase, so refactoring toward patterns lands naturally
JHotDraw is written in a procedural style only

questions.explanation: JHotDraw heavily uses Factory Method, Strategy, Composite, Observer, Decorator, State/Command, Template Method and Adapter, making Kerievsky's pattern-directed refactorings directly applicable. (Refactoring1 p.12)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, a 'CompositeFigure' that contains child 'Figure's exemplifies which pattern that a refactoring might target?
questions.explanation: A CompositeFigure containing Figures is the Composite pattern; Kerievsky offers moves like Replace One/Many Distinctions with Composite. (Refactoring1 p.12)

questions.stem: JHotDraw's interchangeable handles and tools illustrate which pattern that refactoring can introduce?
questions.explanation: Interchangeable behaviour via handles/tools is Strategy; Kerievsky's Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy targets it. (Refactoring1 p.12)

questions.stem: JHotDraw's 'createFigure' factory method is an instance of which pattern?
questions.explanation: createFigure is the Factory Method pattern, one of JHotDraw's well-known pattern roster. (Refactoring1 p.12)

questions.stem: To pull domain logic out of JHotDraw's DrawingView and into the Drawing/Figure model, which Fowler Big Refactoring is the textbook match?
questions.explanation: Separating GUI classes that contain domain logic into domain classes is the Separate Domain from Presentation big refactoring, mirroring JHotDraw's domain/view split. (Refactoring1 p.56)

questions.stem: Which Organizing-Data refactoring supports the GUI/domain synchronisation seen in JHotDraw's MVC design?
questions.explanation: Duplicate Observed Data copies GUI data into a domain object and uses an Observer to keep them in sync, relevant to JHotDraw's MVC split. (Refactoring1 p.27, 189)

questions.stem: Figures notifying their views of changes in JHotDraw is which pattern?
questions.explanation: Figures notifying views is the Observer pattern, supported by Fowler's Duplicate Observed Data refactoring. (Refactoring1 p.27)

questions.stem: Why does the lab require you to work on a feature branch rather than directly on main?

options.text: Because SonarLint only runs on branches
Because refactoring changes behaviour and must be quarantined
Because the GoF patterns require branches
To isolate the refactoring experiment so main stays stable until the work is reviewed/merged

questions.explanation: A feature branch isolates the refactoring work, keeping the mainline stable; the lab prescribes branch -> SonarLint -> refactor -> document. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: After applying Kerievsky refactorings in the lab, what final deliverable does the lab ask for?

options.text: Documentation of the refactoring strategy in the portfolio
A rewrite of JHotDraw from scratch

questions.explanation: The lab closes by asking you to document the chosen refactoring strategy in your portfolio. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: A metric report lists a unit: LOC=15, branch points=2, parameters=3, duplication=0. Against G1-G4, how does it fare?

options.text: Passes all four (15<=15 LOC, 2<=4 branch pts, 3<=4 params, no duplication)

questions.explanation: 15 LOC is at the limit (pass), 2 branch points pass G2, 3 params pass G4, zero duplication passes G3 — fully compliant. (BetterCode p.10, 16, 28)

questions.stem: Report: a unit has LOC=15, 4 branch points, 4 parameters, no duplication. How does it score against G1-G4?

options.text: Passes all four; each metric sits exactly at its threshold

questions.explanation: All four limits are inclusive: 15 LOC, 4 branch points, and 4 parameters each sit at the threshold and pass, with no duplication. (BetterCode p.7, 13, 25)

questions.stem: SonarLint reports a method containing one 'if', one 'for', and one '&&'. What is its cyclomatic complexity, and does it pass G2?
questions.explanation: Branch points: if + for + && = 3; CC = 3 + 1 = 4, which is within G2's CC<=5 (<=4 branch points). (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A unit has these branch constructs: two 'if', one 'case', one 'for', one '||'. Compute CC and judge G2.
questions.explanation: Branch points = 2 if + 1 case + 1 for + 1 || = 5; CC = 6; G2 caps branch points at 4 (CC<=5), so it fails. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A module shows fan-in = 7 on the coupling table. What rating does it get?
questions.explanation: Fan-in 7 falls in the 1-10 band, rated Excellent for modifiability/testability. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: A module shows fan-in = 30. Which band and rating?
questions.explanation: Fan-in 30 is in the 21-50 band, rated Difficult. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: A system reports 9 top-level components, but one component is roughly ten times the size of each of the others. Does it satisfy G7?

options.text: Yes: 9 components is all G7 needs
Yes, because more components is always better
No: G7 requires both ~9 components AND approximately equal size; the size imbalance fails it

questions.explanation: G7 demands both the count (close to 9, 6-12) and roughly equal component sizes; a 10x-larger component violates the balance requirement. (BetterCode p.42)

questions.stem: Metric diff: method 'getVariableToTypeMapping' has LOC=15, params=2, 1 branch point, and 10 lines duplicated with another method. Which guideline does it violate?

options.text: G3 (Write Code Once) — the duplicated block exceeds the 6-line threshold

questions.explanation: LOC=15, params=2, 1 branch point all pass; the 10-line duplicate exceeds G3's >=6-line block threshold, so G3 is violated. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: SonarLint reports an identical 8-line block copied across two methods. Which BetterCode guideline does this breach, and on what basis?

options.text: G1, because each method is too long
G3, because the duplicated block is >= 6 lines
G4, because of too many parameters
None; 8 lines is below the limit

questions.explanation: G3 counts a duplicated block as a violation at >=6 lines; an 8-line shared block crosses that threshold. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: A shared 5-line block appears in two methods. Does it violate G3's duplication threshold?

options.text: No — 5 lines is below the >=6-line duplicated-block threshold
No — G3 has no line threshold

questions.explanation: G3 flags duplicated blocks of >=6 lines; a 5-line block is below the threshold and does not violate it. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: Code review finds the literal 86400 hard-coded inside a method body. Which G10 'How To' rule does it break?
questions.explanation: An unexplained literal like 86400 violates the 'no magic constants' clean-code rule under G10. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: A reviewer finds a large block of commented-out source. Which G10 'How To' rule does it violate?
questions.explanation: Commented-out source code violates the 'no code in comments' G10 rule. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: An unreachable private method that nothing calls is flagged. Which G10 'How To' rule applies?
questions.explanation: An uncalled, unreachable method is dead code, violating the 'no dead code' G10 rule. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: Worked example: 'clearAllMatches(String input, String word)' is 15 LOC, contains a while and a for, and has 2 parameters. Score it against G1, G2, G4.

options.text: Passes all three (15 LOC, CC=3 from 2 branch points, 2 params)

questions.explanation: 15 LOC passes G1; while+for = 2 branch points -> CC=3 passes G2; 2 params passes G4. (BetterCode p.10, 16, 28)

questions.stem: Reading the G2 comparison where 'doAutoCompleteLabel' has 3 branch points and 'isMultiPartForm' has 4, which method(s) satisfy Guideline 2?
questions.explanation: G2 allows up to 4 branch points inclusively, so both 3 and 4 branch points pass. (BetterCode p.17)

questions.stem: Symptom: a method has eight parameters in its signature. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: Too many parameters is the Long Parameter List smell, addressed by Introduce Parameter Object or Replace Parameter with Method. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Symptom: identical logic copy-pasted into three classes. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: The same structure in more than one place is Duplicated Code; the Rule of Three triggers its removal. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Symptom: a library class you cannot edit is missing two methods you need. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: A library class missing needed methods is Incomplete Library Class, fixed by Introduce Foreign Method/Local Extension. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Symptom: two classes do the same job but expose different method names/signatures. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: Same job, different interfaces is Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces (Kerievsky: Unify Interfaces with Adapter). (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Before: a public field accessed directly across the codebase. After: it is private with get/set accessors. Name the refactoring.
questions.explanation: Making a public field private and providing accessors is Encapsulate Field. (Refactoring1 p.28, 206)

questions.stem: Before: an array where index 0 means name and index 1 means age. After: an object with 'name' and 'age' fields. Name the refactoring.
questions.explanation: An array whose elements mean different things is replaced via Replace Array with Object. (Refactoring1 p.28, 186)

questions.stem: BEST refactoring for a method whose name describes it as clearly as its one-line body does?
questions.explanation: When the body is as clear as the name, Inline Method folds it into callers and removes it. (Refactoring1 p.12, 117)

questions.stem: BEST refactoring for two subclass constructors with near-identical bodies?
questions.explanation: Near-identical subclass constructors are unified by Pull Up Constructor Body into a superclass constructor. (Refactoring1 p.43, 325)

questions.stem: BEST refactoring when a superclass and its subclass are barely different?
questions.explanation: A superclass and subclass that are almost the same are merged via Collapse Hierarchy (a Lazy-Class-in-a-hierarchy fix). (Refactoring1 p.46, 344)

questions.stem: BEST refactoring when several clients use the same subset of a class's interface?
questions.explanation: A shared interface subset is captured via Extract Interface. (Refactoring1 p.45, 341)

questions.stem: BEST big refactoring for an inheritance hierarchy doing two jobs at once (e.g. Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies)?
questions.explanation: An over-loaded hierarchy is split into two via Tease Apart Inheritance, using delegation between them. (Refactoring1 p.54)

questions.stem: BEST Kerievsky refactoring to remove a large class's conditional dispatcher by introducing Command objects?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command turns dispatch branches into Command objects, a fix for Large Class/Switch Statements. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: Inline Method and Extract Method are inverses. When is Inline Method the right direction?

options.text: When a method's body is as clear as its name, so the indirection adds nothing
When a method is too long to read
When a fragment should become a named helper
When a method envies another class

questions.explanation: Inline Method removes needless indirection when the body is already as clear as the name; Extract Method is the opposite move. (Refactoring1 p.12, 117)

questions.stem: Replace Delegation with Inheritance vs Replace Inheritance with Delegation: when do you use the former?

options.text: When a subclass refuses its bequest
When you keep writing many simple delegations for the entire interface
When a chain of getters appears

questions.explanation: If you find yourself delegating the whole interface, Replace Delegation with Inheritance makes the delegator a subclass; the inverse handles Refused Bequest. (Refactoring1 p.50, 355)

questions.stem: G1 (short units) and G8 (small codebase) both concern size. How do their levels differ?

options.text: G1 is System level; G8 is Unit level
G1 is Unit-level (15 LOC per unit); G8 is System-level (whole codebase size in man-months)

questions.explanation: G1 limits each unit at the Unit level; G8 constrains the whole codebase at the System level. (BetterCode p.5, 7, 48)

questions.stem: 'Towards a pattern' vs 'away from a pattern' in Kerievsky: what does the existence of both directions imply?

options.text: Only Fowler refactorings move toward patterns
A pattern can be reached or removed by behaviour-preserving steps; over-application is a smell

questions.explanation: Kerievsky's Refactoring Directions list moves both toward and away from each pattern, since over-applying a pattern is itself a smell. (HighLevelRefactoring p.21-22)

questions.stem: Consequence of leaving unit-level smells behind after finishing a change (ignoring G10)?

options.text: The code is not clean and therefore less maintainable; G10 says leave no smells behind

questions.explanation: G10 ('write clean code') requires leaving no code smells behind after development; ignoring it lowers maintainability. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Consequence of tightly coupling top-level components (violating G6)?

options.text: The components become hard to test and change in isolation

questions.explanation: G6's example contrasts loosely-coupled components (easy to test) with tightly-coupled ones (hard to test); tight coupling raises change/test cost. (BetterCode p.40)

questions.stem: If you Replace Conditional with Polymorphism but the subclasses each return only a constant, what better refactoring may now apply?

options.text: Replace Subclass with Fields, since the subclasses vary only by constant data

questions.explanation: Subclasses differing only in constant-returning methods are better collapsed via Replace Subclass with Fields. (Refactoring1 p.30, 232)

questions.stem: A unit passes G1, G2, G4 but contains a 7-line block duplicated elsewhere. What is the net compliance verdict?

options.text: Non-compliant overall, because it still violates G3 (duplication >= 6 lines)

questions.explanation: Passing some guidelines doesn't excuse others; a 7-line duplicate violates G3 regardless of G1/G2/G4. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a SIG guideline at the Unit level?
questions.explanation: G6 (Couple Components Loosely) is a Component-level guideline; G1, G2, G4 are Unit-level. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a refactoring in Fowler's Composing Methods category?
questions.explanation: Move Method is in Moving Features Between Objects; Composing Methods holds Extract/Inline Method and Replace Method with Method Object. (Refactoring1 p.11-13)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of Kerievsky's two refactoring heuristics?

options.text: (only Automation First and Client First exist)

questions.explanation: Kerievsky's two heuristics are Automation First and Client First; 'Threshold First' is not one of them. (HighLevelRefactoring p.20)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a motivation BetterCode gives for automated tests (G9)?

options.text: They make code predictable and document it
They help you write better code

questions.explanation: G9's motivations are repeatable testing, efficient development, predictable code, documentation, and writing better code — not runtime speed. (BetterCode p.54)

questions.stem: You find a class that does nothing but hold a value and expose get/set for it, with no behaviour. As maintainer, what is the likely smell and a fitting move?

options.text: Data Class; move behaviour onto it or inline it where appropriate

questions.explanation: An anaemic field-bag with only accessors is a Data Class; you give it behaviour or fold it in. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: A 'PasteCommand' subclass throws UnsupportedOperationException for most inherited methods. As reviewer, which smell do you cite and which refactoring?

options.text: Refused Bequest; Replace Inheritance with Delegation

questions.explanation: Rejecting most of the inherited contract is Refused Bequest; Replace Inheritance with Delegation removes the bad inheritance. (Refactoring1 p.49, 352)

questions.stem: Before adding undo to a JHotDraw command, you first extract a shared 'execute' skeleton so the new code fits. No behaviour changes yet. Name the phase and its goal.

options.text: Prefactoring; turn a hard change into an easy one

questions.explanation: Restructuring before the functional change, adding no behaviour, to ease the addition is Prefactoring. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Finishing a feature, you notice your new code duplicated a 9-line block. Following the Boy-Scout rule, what do you do before concluding?

options.text: Leave it; it's the next person's job
Add a comment explaining the duplication
Postfactor: extract the shared block so no smell is left behind

questions.explanation: Postfactoring applies the Boy-Scout rule and G10: extract the duplicate so you leave no smell behind. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: SonarLint flags a method with constructs: one 'for', one 'if', one 'case'. Compute CC and verdict for G2.
questions.explanation: for + if + case = 3 branch points; CC = 4, within G2's CC<=5. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A method header has 5 parameters and the body is 12 LOC with 2 branch points and no duplication. Which guideline does it violate?
questions.explanation: 12 LOC passes G1, 2 branch points pass G2, no duplication passes G3; 5 parameters exceed G4's limit of 4. (BetterCode p.25)

questions.stem: A module shows fan-in = 18. Which band and rating?
questions.explanation: Fan-in 18 falls in the 11-20 band, rated Good. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: A system has 4 top-level components of roughly equal size. Does it satisfy G7's count requirement?

options.text: No: the count should be close to 9 (6-12); 4 is too few

questions.explanation: G7 wants the number of top-level components close to 9 (between 6 and 12); 4 is below that range. (BetterCode p.42)

questions.stem: The G3 fix brings the second method down to 15 LOC, 1 parameter, 1 branch point using 'functionHeader.copy()' plus a shared helper. Which two refactorings produced that result?

options.text: Extract Method plus Introduce Parameter Object
Replace Conditional with Polymorphism plus Decompose Conditional

questions.explanation: Factoring the duplicate into a shared helper (Extract Method) and passing a copied object (Introduce Parameter Object) removes the duplication. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: Kerievsky's 'algebra of refactoring' frames patterns as word problems. What is the practical takeaway about HOW you reach a pattern?

options.text: Memorise one canonical implementation per pattern
Express the design problem as an ordered sequence of small behaviour-preserving steps
Apply the pattern in a single atomic rewrite

questions.explanation: The algebra metaphor means you solve design problems through sequences of small behaviour-preserving refactorings, not a single canonical edit. (HighLevelRefactoring p.8-10)

questions.stem: Where do BOTH Prefactoring and Postfactoring sit relative to Concept Location and Impact Analysis?

options.text: Prefactoring before, Postfactoring before Concept Location
After them — you locate and analyse first, then prefactor, actualize, postfactor

questions.explanation: Concept Location and Impact Analysis precede the refactoring bookends; the spine is Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Within a composite refactoring toward Strategy, what must remain true after EACH intermediate step?

options.text: Only that the final pattern is present
That a new feature was added
Observable behaviour is unchanged and the system still works

questions.explanation: Every step of a composite refactoring is behaviour-preserving, keeping the system working throughout (the discipline of small steps). (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Postfactoring is just Actualization done late.' Correct it.

options.text: Postfactoring is behaviour-preserving cleanup after Actualization; Actualization adds behaviour

questions.explanation: Postfactoring removes smells without changing behaviour; only Actualization adds functionality. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'a module with fan-in 11 is still Excellent.' What is correct?

options.text: No — 11 falls in the 11-20 'Good' band, not the 1-10 'Excellent' band

questions.explanation: The Excellent band is 1-10; fan-in 11 starts the Good band. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Compose Method is a Fowler low-level refactoring.' What is correct?

options.text: Compose Method is a Kerievsky refactoring (for Long Method); Fowler's analog is Extract Method
Compose Method is a SIG guideline

questions.explanation: Compose Method is in Kerievsky's catalog (Long Method fix); Fowler's low-level cousin is Extract Method. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: Which BetterCode guideline lives at the Module level?
questions.explanation: G5 (Separate Concerns in Modules) is the sole Module-level guideline; G1 is Unit, G6 is Component, G9 is System. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: Which guideline operates at the System level alongside Automated Tests (G9)?
questions.explanation: G8 and G9 are both System-level; G4/G2 are Unit, G5 is Module. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: Kerievsky's catalog fixes 'Combinatorial Explosion' by moving toward which pattern?
questions.explanation: Combinatorial Explosion is refactored via Replace Implicit Language with Interpreter, toward the Interpreter pattern. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, 'Indecent Exposure' is fixed by moving toward which pattern?
questions.explanation: Indecent Exposure is addressed by Encapsulate Classes with Factory, hiding classes behind a Factory. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, 'Solution Sprawl' is fixed by which refactoring?
questions.explanation: Solution Sprawl (creation knowledge scattered) is consolidated via Move Creation Knowledge to Factory. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: Which Kerievsky refactoring addresses Duplicated Code by introducing a Template Method?
questions.explanation: Form Template Method (205) is Kerievsky's Duplicated-Code fix that introduces a Template Method. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12)

questions.stem: A 'SelectionTool' method reaches across into 'Drawing' to read private-ish state through several getters in a long chain. Best scenario fix?

options.text: Hide Delegate to shorten the chain, or Move Method if the logic belongs on Drawing

questions.explanation: A long navigation chain plus misplaced logic suggests Hide Delegate (shorten) or Move Method (relocate) depending on dominant smell. (Refactoring1 p.15-18)

questions.stem: You see the same 11-line block in 'save()' and 'export()' of one class. Most appropriate scenario action?

options.text: Extract Method into a shared private helper both call

questions.explanation: Duplicated Code within a class is removed by Extract Method into a shared helper. (Refactoring1 p.11, 110)

questions.stem: Mid-task you realize the area you must change is too tangled to edit safely, so you stop and clean it first. Which Fowler trigger is this, and which phase?

options.text: 'Refactor As You Do a Code Review' — Postfactoring
'Refactor When You Need to Fix a Bug' — Verification

questions.explanation: Cleaning before injecting the new function is Fowler's 'Refactor When You Add Function', which the course maps to Prefactoring. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: A bug hides in a 50-line conditional jungle. Per Fowler's 'when to refactor' triggers, what do you do first?

options.text: Refactor the area clear enough to see the bug, then fix it
Patch the bug without touching structure

questions.explanation: Fowler's 'Refactor When You Need to Fix a Bug' says make the area clear enough to see the defect first. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a Fowler refactoring in the Simplifying Conditional Expressions category?

options.text: Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses

questions.explanation: Introduce Parameter Object is in Making Method Calls Simpler; the conditionals category holds Decompose, Consolidate, Guard Clauses, Polymorphism, Null Object. (Refactoring1 p.32-36)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the four SIG levels?
questions.explanation: 'Subsystem' is not a SIG level; the four are Unit, Module, Component, System. (BetterCode p.9)

questions.stem: Which statement is NOT true of behaviour-preserving transformations?

options.text: Same inputs yield the same outputs
They can be verified by tests
They may change outputs for some inputs if the code is cleaner

questions.explanation: Behaviour preservation forbids changed outputs for any input; if outputs change, it is not a refactoring. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a Kerievsky refactoring listed for Long Method?
questions.explanation: Long Method's Kerievsky fixes include Compose Method, Move Accumulation to Collecting Parameter/Visitor, Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy, and Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command — not 'Dispatcher with Strategy'. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: Encapsulate Field vs Self Encapsulate Field: what is the distinction?

options.text: Encapsulate Field hides a public field behind accessors; Self Encapsulate Field routes even internal access through accessors
Self Encapsulate Field makes a field public

questions.explanation: Encapsulate Field privatizes a public field with accessors; Self Encapsulate Field additionally has the class use those accessors internally. (Refactoring1 p.24-28)

questions.stem: Extract Subclass vs Extract Superclass: which targets which situation?

options.text: Extract Subclass for features used only in some instances; Extract Superclass for features common to two classes
They are inverses of Inline Method

questions.explanation: Extract Subclass isolates instance-specific features (e.g. Temporary Field); Extract Superclass hoists shared features from siblings. (Refactoring1 p.43-45)

questions.stem: G3 (no duplication) and G4 (small interfaces) both live at the Unit level but measure different things. What does each count?

options.text: G3 counts parameters; G4 counts duplicate lines
G3 counts duplicated blocks (>=6 lines); G4 counts parameters per unit (<=4)

questions.explanation: G3's measure is duplicated blocks of >=6 lines; G4's is the parameter count per unit header. (BetterCode p.23, 25)

questions.stem: Symptom: a method dives through 'a.b().c().d().e()' to reach what it needs. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: Long transitive navigation a.b().c().d() is Message Chains, fixed by Hide Delegate. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Symptom: a class exists that does almost nothing and no longer earns its keep. Name the smell.
questions.explanation: A class not doing enough to justify maintenance is a Lazy Class (fix: Inline Class / Collapse Hierarchy). (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Before: client code holds a reference to a delegate and calls it directly in many places. After: the server exposes wrapper methods and clients stop touching the delegate. Name the refactoring.
questions.explanation: Wrapping the delegate behind server methods so clients stop calling it is Hide Delegate. (Refactoring1 p.18, 157)

questions.stem: Reverse-id: a smell fixed by 'Replace State-Altering Conditionals with State' is which Kerievsky/Fowler smell category?
questions.explanation: Replace State-Altering Conditionals with State targets Conditional Complexity and Primitive Obsession by introducing the State pattern. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12-13)

questions.stem: If you Extract Method aggressively until a class has dozens of tiny one-line methods, what risk emerges?

options.text: Over-extraction can hurt readability; refactoring is about balance, not maximizing method count

questions.explanation: Refactoring optimizes understandability; blindly maximizing tiny methods can reduce clarity, so judgment governs how far to go. (Refactoring1 p.3-4)

questions.stem: Consequence of accumulating smells without ever refactoring, in design terms?

options.text: The design decays under accumulated changes, raising the cost of future change

questions.explanation: Fowler's first reason to refactor is that without it design decays under change; smells left unattended raise future change cost. (Refactoring1 p.4)

questions.stem: A component grows until it hides almost no code (most modules are externally callable). Consequence for G6?

options.text: Lower component independence — the hidden-code percentage drops, worsening loose coupling

questions.explanation: G6 measures the weighted percentage of hidden code; if little is hidden, component independence and loose coupling worsen. (BetterCode p.39)

questions.stem: BEST refactoring when a method passes a computed result that the receiver could compute itself?
questions.explanation: If the receiver can recompute the value, Replace Parameter with Method drops the parameter. (Refactoring1 p.40, 292)

questions.stem: BEST refactoring when several methods do the same thing but with different hard-coded literals?
questions.explanation: Parameterize Method merges near-identical methods into one that takes the differing value as a parameter (a Duplicated Code variant). (Refactoring1 p.40, 283)

questions.stem: BEST big refactoring for procedural code with data records and free-standing functions you want to objectify?
questions.explanation: Convert Procedural Design to Objects turns data records into objects and moves behaviour onto them. (Refactoring1 p.55)

questions.stem: BEST Kerievsky move to unify two classes that do the same job but expose different interfaces?
questions.explanation: Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces is reconciled by Unify Interfaces with Adapter. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Encapsulate Collection should return the live internal list for performance.' What is correct?

options.text: It should return a read-only view and expose add/remove methods, not the live collection

questions.explanation: Encapsulate Collection returns a read-only view to protect the internal collection, with add/remove methods for controlled mutation. (Refactoring1 p.30, 208)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'G7 just wants as many components as possible.' What is correct?

options.text: It wants the count close to 9 (6-12) AND roughly equal sizes — more is not always better

questions.explanation: G7 balances both number (~9, 6-12) and uniform size; runaway component counts are not the goal. (BetterCode p.42)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'writing tests slows you down with no design benefit.' What does BetterCode claim?

options.text: Correct: tests have no design benefit
Writing tests makes you write better code and documents it

questions.explanation: G9's motivations include that writing tests makes you write better code and that tests document the code. (BetterCode p.54)

questions.stem: Which phase actually adds the new functionality, distinguishing it from the two refactoring bookends?
questions.explanation: Actualization is the only one of these that changes observable behaviour by adding functionality; Pre/Postfactoring are behaviour-preserving. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Kerievsky's 21-session 'study sequence' orders the refactorings for what purpose?

options.text: Learning — a pedagogical ordering of the refactorings

questions.explanation: The deck's study sequence orders the refactorings as a learning path, not a runtime or metric tool. (HighLevelRefactoring p.14-15)

questions.stem: After SonarLint flags smells in the lab, but BEFORE applying refactorings, what should already be done?

options.text: A feature branch should be created to isolate the work
The fix should be merged to main

questions.explanation: The lab creates the feature branch first, then runs SonarLint and applies refactorings before documenting. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: SonarLint: a method has constructs 'if', 'if', '&&', 'for'. Compute CC and verdict for G2.
questions.explanation: if + if + && + for = 4 branch points; CC = 5, exactly at G2's ceiling, so it passes. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A module shows fan-in = 51. Band and rating?
questions.explanation: Fan-in 51 exceeds 50, landing in the Impossible band for modifiability/testability. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: Report: unit LOC=16, branch points=3, params=2, duplication=0. Which guideline does it violate?
questions.explanation: 16 LOC exceeds G1's 15-line limit; branch points (3<=4) and params (2<=4) pass, no duplication. (BetterCode p.7)

questions.stem: Worked example metric: a unit is reported with CC=3 from a while and a for. How many branch points does that imply?

options.text: 2 branch points (while + for), since CC = branch points + 1

questions.explanation: CC = 3 means branch points = CC - 1 = 2, matching the while + for. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A method body contains an identifier named 'theNumberOfCustomersWhoHaveActiveSubscriptionsThisMonth'. Which G10 rule does it strain?
questions.explanation: An excessively long identifier violates the 'no long identifier names' G10 clean-code rule. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: A catch block that swallows the exception and does nothing violates which G10 rule?
questions.explanation: Silently swallowing an exception is a badly handled exception, violating that G10 clean-code rule. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: JHotDraw uses figure decorations layered onto figures. Which pattern does this illustrate, and which Kerievsky move targets it?

options.text: Strategy; Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy

questions.explanation: Layered figure decorations are the Decorator pattern; Kerievsky's Move Embellishment to Decorator introduces it. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12)

questions.stem: JHotDraw's undoable commands and tool modes illustrate which patterns refactoring can introduce?
questions.explanation: Undoable commands map to Command, and tool modes to State — both Kerievsky targets via Replace ...Conditionals with Command/State. (Refactoring1 p.12)

questions.stem: In the lab, you find a Large Class in JHotDraw with a big conditional dispatcher. Which Kerievsky refactoring is most apt?
questions.explanation: A Large Class driven by a conditional dispatcher is refactored via Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: What is the inverse refactoring of Hide Delegate?
questions.explanation: Hide Delegate's inverse is Remove Middle Man; one hides the delegate, the other exposes it. (Refactoring1 p.18, 160)

questions.stem: Over-applying a design pattern most closely matches which Fowler smell?
questions.explanation: Adding generality/patterns for a need that may never come is Speculative Generality. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Which guideline carries the 'do not copy code' rule?
questions.explanation: G3 ('Write Code Once') forbids copying code, with a >=6-line duplicated-block measure. (BetterCode p.20)

questions.stem: Which guideline limits parameters per unit to at most four?
questions.explanation: G4 ('Keep Unit Interfaces Small') caps parameters at 4. (BetterCode p.25)

questions.stem: A composite refactoring 'suggests non-obvious design directions.' Which benefit category is this?

options.text: One of Kerievsky's three benefits of composite refactorings

questions.explanation: Suggesting non-obvious directions is one of the three composite-refactoring benefits (with overall plan and pattern insight). (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: Kerievsky's 'Automation First' heuristic is summarized by which metaphor?

options.text: Patterns are word problems; refactoring is algebra
Manual refactorings are dirt roads; automated ones are highways

questions.explanation: Automation First: prefer tool-automated refactorings ('highways') over manual ones ('dirt roads'). (HighLevelRefactoring p.20)

questions.stem: Kerievsky's 'Client First' heuristic advises what?

options.text: Start refactoring from a client of the smelly code to find a simpler automated path
Refactor the server class first always
Add the new feature before refactoring

questions.explanation: Client First: begin from a client of the smelly code to discover a simpler, automatable refactoring path. (HighLevelRefactoring p.20)
