questions.stem: The phrase 'patterns are the word problems of programming; refactoring is its ___' completes with which word?
questions.explanation: Kerievsky's metaphor: patterns are the word problems and refactoring is the algebra. (HighLevelRefactoring p.8-9)

questions.stem: BetterCode operationalizes the 10 guidelines through which named tool?
questions.explanation: BetterCode's measurable guidelines are operationalized by bettercodehub.com. (BetterCode p.3)

questions.stem: Which Fowler Big Refactoring most directly maps to separating JHotDraw's domain logic out of GUI classes?
questions.explanation: Separate Domain from Presentation pulls domain logic out of GUI classes, matching JHotDraw's MVC layering. (Refactoring1 p.56)

questions.stem: A unit reports LOC=15, branch points=5, params=4, duplication=0. Which single guideline does it violate?
questions.explanation: 15 LOC passes G1, 4 params pass G4, no duplication passes G3; 5 branch points (CC=6) violate G2. (BetterCode p.13, 16)

questions.stem: Which Fowler refactoring directly implements the Template Method pattern?
questions.explanation: Form Template Method aligns subclass step methods so the skeleton can be pulled up — implementing Template Method. (Refactoring1 p.47, 345)

questions.stem: Which guideline pairs 'leave no code smells behind' with the Postfactoring discipline?
questions.explanation: G10 ('leave no smells behind after development work') is literally the Postfactoring discipline. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Which is the canonical fix for the Duplicated Code smell when the duplicate sits in two sibling subclasses?
questions.explanation: Duplicate logic across siblings is hoisted via Extract Superclass or unified with Form Template Method. (Refactoring1 p.45-47)

questions.stem: In this lecture's framing, which two phases use the SAME catalogs and metrics, differing only in timing?
questions.explanation: Pre- and Postfactoring both draw on the Fowler/Kerievsky catalogs and SIG metrics; only their position around Actualization differs. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: A method returns the boolean result of a query but also flips a dirty flag. Which principle does Separate Query from Modifier restore, and how?

options.text: Single Responsibility; by deleting the flag
Command-Query Separation; by splitting into a pure query and a separate modifier

questions.explanation: Separate Query from Modifier restores Command-Query Separation by splitting the side-effecting query into two methods. (Refactoring1 p.39, 279)

questions.stem: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism leans on which OO principle to dispatch behaviour by type?
questions.explanation: The refactoring moves each conditional leg into an overriding subclass method, relying on subtype polymorphism. (Refactoring1 p.34, 255)

questions.stem: A Data Class with only fields and accessors most clearly under-uses which OO idea?
questions.explanation: A Data Class separates behaviour from data, under-using encapsulation (objects should bundle behaviour with state). (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Of the four code edits below, which one fails the behaviour-preservation test and so is NOT a refactoring?

options.text: Adding a caching layer that changes which exception is thrown on failure
Splitting a long method into two private helpers
Moving a field to its primary user

questions.explanation: Changing which exception is thrown alters observable behaviour, so it is a modification, not a refactoring. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: Reading a guideline/level/threshold summary table, which single row is internally consistent (all three fields correct)?

options.text: G3 Write Code Once / Component / 4 branch points

questions.explanation: G1 is Unit-level with a 15-LOC threshold; the other rows mismatch level or threshold. (BetterCode p.5, 7)

questions.stem: A reviewer marks a 13-LOC method with 1 branch point and 3 params as 'needs refactoring for size.' Is that justified by the SIG thresholds?

options.text: No — it passes G1 (<=15), G2 (<=4), and G4 (<=4); no size threshold is breached

questions.explanation: 13 LOC, 1 branch point, and 3 params are all within G1/G2/G4 limits, so a size-based objection is unfounded. (BetterCode p.7, 13, 25)

questions.stem: You must add a feature to a method that is already 14 LOC; the addition would push it to 20 LOC. Best sequence honoring Prefactoring?

options.text: Just add the code and exceed 15 LOC
Add the code, then delete tests
Inline the method, then add the code
Prefactor by Extract Method to shrink it first, then add the feature so each unit stays <=15 LOC

questions.explanation: Prefactoring (Extract Method) makes room before Actualization so the final units respect G1's 15-LOC limit. (Refactoring1 p.5; BetterCode p.7)

questions.stem: Kerievsky shows 'Hello World' as Strategy, Factory, and Main to make which point?

options.text: There are many ways to implement a pattern; no single canonical implementation

questions.explanation: The three Hello World variants illustrate that a pattern has many valid implementations, not one canonical form. (HighLevelRefactoring p.18-19)

questions.stem: The Jefferson-to-Franklin edit of the Declaration ('self-evident' for 'sacred and undeniable') is the deck's metaphor for what?
questions.explanation: The revision metaphor stands for refining expression through small improving steps that preserve meaning — like refactoring. (HighLevelRefactoring p.6)
